food_subgroup: Legumes and pulses — cooked lentil dish with unleavened flatbread

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Yellow Dal Tadka with Chapati; closest_parent is lentil food product (FOODON_03311434).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked lentils as closest parent; no specific code for dal tadka preparation with chapati.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229961001 covers lentil as a substance; no specific concept for dal tadka dish with chapati.

main_ingredients: yellow lentils (toor dal or moong dal)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving consists of approximately 250 g of yellow dal tadka (one bowl) served alongside two medium chapati (approximately 150 g combined), totalling around 400 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Dal and Cereal Preparations
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are derived by combining ICMR-NIN data for cooked toor dal (arhar dal) and whole wheat chapati in a 62.5:37.5 weight ratio (250 g dal to 150 g chapati per 400 g serving), then normalised to per-100 g. Ghee tempering contribution to fat and saturated fat is incorporated based on ICMR-NIN ghee values at approximately 5 g ghee per 250 g dal. Values represent a composite dish estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Lentils cooked and Chapati/Roti whole wheat
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 40 is a weighted composite estimate: cooked yellow lentils (toor/moong dal) have a GI of approximately 29–38 per University of Sydney GI Database; whole wheat chapati has a GI of approximately 52–62. A weighted average based on the 62.5:37.5 dal-to-chapati ratio yields approximately 40, classified as low (GI ≤ 55). Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 90 g carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 = 36.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from salt and spice tempering
hypertension.guidance: A 400 g serving provides approximately 840 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt preparation and limit portion size to one chapati to keep sodium intake within the 1500–2000 mg daily target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index driven by high-fibre lentils
type2_diabetes.guidance: The composite GI of approximately 40 and high dietary fibre content (3.1 g per 100 g) support gradual glucose release, making this dish suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes when chapati portions are controlled to one piece per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Soluble fibre and plant protein from lentils offset by ghee-derived saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lentils provide soluble fibre and plant protein beneficial for cardiovascular health, but ghee contributes saturated fat (1.6 g per 100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should request the dish prepared with minimal ghee or substituted with a small amount of canola oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yellow Dal Tadka with Chapati is a classic North Indian vegetarian meal consisting of two components: a bowl of cooked yellow lentils (typically toor dal or moong dal) finished with a hot ghee-based tempering (tadka) of cumin seeds, dried red chillies, garlic, and turmeric, served alongside two soft whole wheat flatbreads (chapati). The lentils are first pressure-cooked or simmered until soft, then seasoned with salt and turmeric. The tadka is prepared separately by heating ghee until smoking, then blooming whole spices and aromatics before pouring the sizzling mixture over the dal, which releases a characteristic nutty, smoky aroma. Chapati is rolled thin from whole wheat dough and cooked on a dry tawa (griddle) until lightly charred. The combined dish delivers a balanced macronutrient profile: approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with 6.8 g protein, 22.5 g carbohydrate, and 3.1 g dietary fibre. The low glycemic index of approximately 40 makes it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium content is moderate at 210 mg per 100 g and should be monitored for hypertension. This dish is a staple across Indian households and restaurants in Singapore, commonly served as a lunch or dinner main course. It is vegetarian and halal-certified in most Indian restaurant contexts. Nutritionally, it is a good source of plant protein, iron (2.1 mg per 100 g), folate (45 mcg DFE per 100 g), and complex carbohydrates, making it a nutritionally dense everyday meal.

confidence_reason: No exact match for Yellow Dal Tadka with Chapati as a composite dish in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked toor dal and whole wheat chapati entries combined in a 62.5:37.5 weight ratio with ghee tempering adjustment. GI is a weighted composite estimate from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for cooked lentils and whole wheat chapati.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this dish category. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this dish category. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 as this is a plant-based vegetarian dish containing no animal flesh; trace amounts may be present from ghee but are below detection threshold — confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN plant food entries. Trans fat is 0.0 as ghee contains negligible industrially produced trans fats; naturally occurring ruminant trans fats are below the reporting threshold in ICMR-NIN data — confirmed zero per source. Food group retained as Meat and alternatives per input classification, consistent with the legume/pulse category used in Singapore dietary guidelines where lentils serve as a protein alternative. The composite dish weight ratio (250 g dal : 150 g chapati) is based on the input description specifying 2 chapati pieces and a standard restaurant bowl portion. GI category is low (GI = 40, which is ≤ 55 per Veritas threshold rules).
